---
title: "A complete data-grounded enzyme- and temperature-constrained model of *Escherichia coli* thermal performance: construction, validation and what sets the curve"
date: today
abstract: |
  We assemble a single, complete, data-grounded enzyme- and temperature-constrained
  genome-scale model (etc-GEM) of *Escherichia coli* (GECKO eciML1515) and use it to
  ask what sets a microbial growth thermal performance curve (TPC). The model combines
  a two-state protein-unfolding thermal envelope keyed on grounded per-enzyme melting
  temperatures $T_m$ and optima $T_\text{opt}$ (90 % coverage; DLTKcat where available),
  a shared proteome pool refined into metabolic / biosynthesis / maintenance sectors
  whose split is **set from the measured *E. coli* proteome, matched to both growth
  medium and temperature** (per-medium LB / Glucose / Glycerol series, incl. a 2.7-fold
  heat-stress chaperone ramp). Crucially, **nothing is fit to the growth curve**: both
  the magnitude and the envelope emerge from independent inputs — the pool budget from
  $P_\text{total}\times f_\text{metab}\times\sigma$ (literature protein content ×
  measured metabolic fraction × literature in-vivo saturation), and the activation
  energy from a literature MMRT $\Delta C_p$ prior plus DLTKcat, so the emergent
  $E_a$ (medium- and allocation-dependent, ~0.6–1.0 eV) is fixed by grounded inputs rather than
  tuned — near the independent ~0.85 eV bacterial-growth benchmark by default, and matching the
  observed rich-curve value under the coupled growth law. Validated on **raw absolute growth rate**
  against the one curve measured on the **exact GEM strain** — *E. coli* K-12 MG1655 in
  Brain-Heart-Infusion broth over 7–46 °C (Van Derlinden 2012), predicted a priori under a rich
  medium (availability, uptake unpinned) with the model configured as it will be tuned — the
  emergent model **tracks the curve shape** (rounded optimum 40 °C vs observed 40; rising-limb
  $E_a$ 0.63 eV vs 0.64; hot collapse near 51 vs 46 °C; positive absolute $R^2=0.27$ over the full
  span) but **under-predicts the absolute peak ~2.3-fold a priori** (1.04 vs 2.40 h⁻¹). A labelled
  **Bayesian calibration** then asks what corrections the curve demands of a few global knobs (the
  grounded per-enzyme parameters are not re-fit): it **closes ~89 % of the magnitude gap** (posterior
  peak 2.14 h⁻¹ [1.98, 2.29]) with a modest turnover correction ($k_\text{cat}$ ×1.25) and the
  in-vivo saturation $\sigma$ railed toward its physical 1.0 ceiling (0.867 [0.671, 0.986], above the
  0.4–0.5 literature range), while a melting-temperature shift ($dT_m=-5.6$ K) pulls the hot collapse
  to match the data and $E_a$ holds near the ~0.85 eV benchmark. The residual is the $\sigma\le1$
  **physical ceiling** — near-maximal in-vivo saturation, the fast-grower point — not a structural
  limit (an earlier apparent ~1.55 h⁻¹ ceiling was a proteome double-accounting artefact, removed by
  reconciling two redundant enzyme-mass pools). Dissecting this **tuned** model, an uncertainty-aware
  local elasticity and a global variance decomposition agree on a **clean, feature-specific** division
  of labour: the maximum growth rate $r_\text{max}$ is owned by the magnitude levers
  ($\varphi_\text{magnitude}=0.92$; the in-vivo saturation has an $r_\text{max}$ elasticity $\approx1$),
  the optimum $T_\text{opt}$ and upper limit $CT_\text{max}$ by protein stability / $T_m$
  ($\varphi_\text{envelope}\approx0.99$), and the cold side and activation energy $E_a$ by the kinetic
  envelope, with a negligible interaction. Thermal control is concentrated in a few enzymes while
  **over 99 %** of per-enzyme parameters are unidentifiable from growth alone. All results are
  structural in-silico predictions pending further phenotype/omics calibration.
---

# Background and objectives

A **thermal performance curve (TPC)** is how a rate — here microbial growth rate — varies
with temperature: it rises from a cold limit, peaks at an optimum, and falls to a hot limit.
The temperature dependence of microbial growth, respiration and carbon-use efficiency governs
how microbial communities, and the carbon they cycle, respond to warming, so predicting these
curves matters for climate–carbon models. The broad aim of this work is to **predict such
curves from a genome**.

A cell's growth curve is not a single measured parameter but an emergent outcome of its whole
metabolism operating under a finite, temperature-sensitive supply of enzymes. A natural
mechanistic predictor is therefore an **enzyme- and temperature-constrained genome-scale
metabolic model (etc-GEM)**: a computational reconstruction of an organism's metabolism in
which every reaction requires a temperature-dependent amount of enzyme drawn from a limited
budget, so that supplying it with a genome plus enzyme kinetic and stability parameters yields
a predicted growth rate at any temperature. We build and validate the method on *Escherichia
coli* using **eciML1515**, an enzyme-constrained version (built with the GECKO method
[@Sanchez2017; @Domenzain2022]) of the manually curated iML1515 metabolic reconstruction
[@Monk2017] — the best-curated bacterial model available — as a proving ground before scaling
to a phylogenetically diverse library of isolates.

The in-silico work addresses six objectives:
**O1** reproduce a realistic *E. coli* growth TPC and define its components;
**O2** determine what sets the thermal **envelope** ($T_\text{opt}$, $CT_\text{max}$,
breadth, $E_a$) and whether a few enzymes dominate;
**O3** determine what sets the rate **magnitude** ($r_\text{max}$) — proteome allocation;
**O4** quantify the **separability** of a genome-set envelope from an allocation-set
magnitude — the key hypothesis;
**O5** assess **identifiability** — which parameters are inferable from growth alone;
**O6** ground every input in independent data rather than fitting it to growth.

We describe the **one complete model**, validate it, and run all analyses on it; a
Supplementary ablation quantifies how much each of its layers contributes.

# The complete model

**In plain terms.** The model turns a genome plus enzyme parameters into a predicted growth
rate at any temperature, in four stacked layers. The first says growth requires enzymes and
the cell has a limited budget of them. The second makes each enzyme's efficiency
temperature-dependent — enzymes speed up with warming, then unfold and fail at high
temperature. The third fixes how the enzyme budget is split among broad functional groups,
using measured proteome data, including how that split shifts with growth rate. The fourth is
the epistemic stance: the emergent model is a genuine forward prediction — every number grounded
in independent data, nothing fit to the growth curve — with any calibration kept as a separate,
labelled inverse step. We now describe each layer.

**Layer 1 — metabolic network with an enzyme budget.** We start from iML1515, a manually
curated map of *E. coli* metabolism (its reaction stoichiometry and gene–protein–reaction
rules, GPRs) [@Monk2017], solved by **flux-balance analysis (FBA)** — a linear-programming
method that finds the reaction fluxes maximising growth subject to mass balance and capacity
limits, using cobrapy [@Ebrahim2013]. GECKO [@Sanchez2017; @Domenzain2022] makes it
**enzyme-constrained**: running each reaction $i$ at flux $v_i$ requires an amount of enzyme
$v_i/k_{\text{cat},i}$, where $k_{\text{cat},i}$ (the *turnover number*) is how many reactions
one copy of the enzyme catalyses per second. The total enzyme mass is capped by a shared
**proteome pool**, so the cell must *allocate* a finite protein budget across reactions —
this is what makes allocation a limiting resource and growth a competition for enzyme.

**Layer 2 — a temperature-dependent enzyme envelope (the "thermal envelope").** Each enzyme's
turnover rises with temperature following **macromolecular rate theory (MMRT)** [@Hobbs2013] —
an extension of the Arrhenius law in which the activation energy itself changes with
temperature (a heat-capacity term), giving a curved, single-peaked turnover response rather
than a straight Arrhenius line. Above a threshold the enzyme unfolds: we model the fraction
still in the working (folded) state with a **two-state native$\leftrightarrow$unfolded
equilibrium** (after Li et al. [@Li2021] and the MRes of Madkaikar [@Madkaikar2023]), the
**native fraction** $f_{N,i}(T)$, which collapses from one to zero around the enzyme's
**melting temperature** $T_m$ (the temperature at which half the protein is unfolded). The
effective per-flux cost combines both effects,
$\text{cost}_i(T)=\text{base}_i/[\,\widehat{k}_{\text{cat},i}(T)\cdot f_{N,i}(T)\,]$, so cost
rises both when turnover is slow (cold) and when the enzyme denatures (hot). Per-enzyme $T_m$
come from a measurement of which *E. coli* proteins melt at which temperature (the "melting
proteome") [@Leuenberger2017], and each enzyme's optimum temperature $T_\text{opt}$ from a
sequence-based predictor [@LiEngqvist2019] (**90 % of enzymes grounded** in these data, the
rest set to dataset means); **DLTKcat**, a deep-learning predictor of temperature-dependent
$k_\text{cat}$ [@Qiu2024], refines $T_\text{opt}$ where it fits well. Keying the collapse on
$T_m$ ties the upper thermal limit to protein *stability* and lets it differ from the
rising-limb activation energy. Turnover numbers measured in a test tube (**in vitro**) tend
to overstate the effective rate inside a cell (**in vivo**), which motivates the
data-driven-saturation correction below [@Davidi2016; @Heckmann2020].

**Layer 3 — proteome allocation: measured sectors, a biosynthesis cap, and the bacterial
growth law.** We split the enzyme budget into three broad **proteome sectors** [@Basan2015; @Scott2010]:
a **metabolic** sector ($f_\text{metab}$, the enzymes that carry flux), a **biosynthesis** sector
($f_\text{bio}$, dominated by ribosomes), and a **maintenance** sector ($f_\text{maint}$,
housekeeping and stress proteins). Four things act on this partition.

*Static, measured allocation.* The sector fractions are **taken from the measured *E. coli*
proteome, matched to both growth medium and temperature** (Wang et al. [@Wang2026]; separate
LB / glucose / glycerol series across their measured temperatures), never hand-set or fit to
growth, so the split is condition-specific: the measured ribosome fraction at 37 °C is
$f_\text{bio}\approx0.36$ on rich LB but only $\approx0.19$ on glucose-minimal, so a prediction
run under a rich medium automatically gets a higher biosynthesis cap and grows faster. The default
glucose-minimal split is $f_\text{metab}=0.483$, $f_\text{bio}=0.191$, $f_\text{maint}=0.326$
(maintenance absorbs the chaperone/housekeeping proteins, including a 2.7-fold heat-stress
chaperone rise); the rich (BHI/LB) reference used for validation is
$f_\text{metab}\approx0.28$, $f_\text{bio}\approx0.36$, $f_\text{maint}\approx0.36$ at 37 °C.

*The biosynthesis (translation) cap.* The biosynthesis sector caps how fast biomass can be made —
a translation limit $\kappa\,v_\text{bio}\le f_\text{bio}\,P_\text{tot}$ (@eq-biocap) that binds
alongside the metabolic-pool bound (@eq-pool).

*The growth-rate-dependent growth law.* The static medium-matched fractions above are only the
resting split; the true bacterial growth law is that allocation shifts *with growth rate itself*.
When the coupled, proteome-conserving mode is enabled (Scott 2010 [@Scott2010]), rising growth
moves proteome out of the metabolic sector into ribosomes at a fixed slope —
$f_\text{bio}(\mu)=f_{\text{bio},0}+s\mu$ and $f_\text{metab}(\mu)=f_{\text{metab},0}-s\mu$
(@eq-growthlaw) — so the **ribosome $\leftrightarrow$ metabolism trade-off**, not a static cap,
sets the maximal rate. (Earlier drafts called the medium-matched $f_\text{bio}$ "the growth law";
that was only the static part.)

*Temperature-dependent maintenance.* A **maintenance (NGAM, non-growth-associated maintenance)**
demand — the ATP the cell spends just to stay alive — is included and made to **rise with
temperature** (@eq-ngam), which lifts the hot-side ATP burden and **rounds the curve into a
realistic optimum** rather than a sharp cliff.

**Layer 4 — an emergent a-priori prediction, with calibration kept separate.** The design stance
has two distinct parts. *The emergent model is the prediction.* Every input is grounded in
independent data — the per-enzyme thermal parameters (Layer 2), the measured sector allocation
(Layer 3), and the literature magnitude scalars — and **nothing is fit to the growth curve**. The
rate *magnitude* follows from the enzyme budget $P_\text{metab}=\sigma\,f_\text{metab}\,P^{\text{lit}}$
(@eq-alloc): literature total protein ($P^{\text{lit}}=0.5$ g/gDW), the measured metabolic fraction,
and the average **in-vivo enzyme saturation** ($\sigma=0.45$, an independent literature value
carried with its 0.4–0.5 range [@Davidi2016; @Heckmann2020]) — not the growth-calibrated pool GECKO
ships. The *envelope* emerges too: each enzyme's curvature is a single literature MMRT value
(−4 kJ/mol/K [@Hobbs2013]) refined by DLTKcat, with nothing chosen to hit an activation-energy
target, so the whole-organism activation energy **emerges and can be tested** against an independent
benchmark. How this a-priori curve compares to the exact-strain data — in shape and in absolute
level — is the subject of the Validation section. *Calibration is a separate, labelled step.* On top
of this prior, a **Bayesian inverse analysis** (the global correction knobs of @tbl-knobs) asks what
corrections a measured curve *demands* of the few borrowed or uncertain global knobs — it does
**not** re-fit the grounded per-enzyme parameters; what it demands, and how much of the a-priori gap
it closes, is quantified in the Calibration section, and the tuned model is what the sensitivity,
decomposition and identifiability sections then dissect. Keeping the emergent prediction and the
tuned posterior distinct is the point: it quantifies the gap rather than papering over it.

**Inputs and data provenance — what the model uses for each enzyme, and where it comes from.** The
model is **per-enzyme**: every one of the ~2,560 modelled enzymes carries its own set of inputs,
applied individually (not one global temperature response shared across the proteome). For each
enzyme the model uses four things, from these sources (exactly as implemented):

- **Flux** (how much reaction it carries) — **not measured; predicted** by the flux-balance
  optimisation. It is an *output* of the model, not an input.
- **Turnover $k_\text{cat}(T)$** (how fast one copy works at temperature $T$) — built from the
  enzyme's reference turnover in the **GECKO ecModel** [@Sanchez2017; @Domenzain2022] shaped by a
  temperature response: a macromolecular-rate-theory (MMRT) curvature and, where a fit exists, the
  **DLTKcat** deep-learning predictor of temperature-dependent $k_\text{cat}$ [@Qiu2024].
- **Thermal optimum $T_\text{opt}$ and curvature** (where turnover peaks and how sharply it falls) —
  the optimum is **sequence/ML-predicted** per enzyme (a TOME-style predictor, after Li–Engqvist
  [@LiEngqvist2019]), refined by DLTKcat where it fits; the curvature is a single independent
  literature MMRT $\Delta C_p$ prior [@Hobbs2013] (deliberately *not* fitted, so $E_a$ emerges).
- **Unfolding / melting temperature $T_m$** (where the enzyme denatures, setting the native fraction
  $f_N(T)$) — from the measured ***E. coli* melting proteome** [@Leuenberger2017], applied through
  the two-state native$\leftrightarrow$unfolded formulation of Li et al. [@Li2021] and the MRes of
  Madkaikar [@Madkaikar2023]. Enzymes without a measured value (≈10 %) fall back to the dataset mean.

So, to answer the natural question directly: **yes — the turnover response, the thermal optimum and
the melting temperature are all applied per enzyme individually**, each from the source named above;
only the pool budget scalars ($P_\text{total}$, $\sigma$) and the sector fractions are whole-cell
quantities.

**Enzyme abundance, and the compensation question.** Per-enzyme **abundances are not measured or set
individually.** Each enzyme's proteome mass usage is $\text{flux}/k_\text{cat}(T)\times\text{MW}$,
drawn from a **shared proteome pool** that the optimiser allocates across all enzymes; only at the
coarse **sector** level do we supply measured (medium- and temperature-matched) proteome fractions.
Per-enzyme abundance is therefore an **optimisation outcome, not an input**. This has a subtle
consequence worth stating plainly: as temperature rises and an enzyme's $k_\text{cat}$ falls (or it
begins to unfold), carrying the same flux costs **more** enzyme mass, so the optimiser *does* draw
more of the pool toward those steps — an implicit, mass-balance "compensation" — until the pool
binds and growth is throttled. But this is **least-cost allocation under a fixed budget, not
regulatory sensing**: there is no mechanism that detects a falling product and up-regulates a gene.
The distinction matters and is a genuine limitation — the model captures the *demand* side of
thermal compensation (more enzyme needed when catalysis is poor) but not active feedback regulation.

# Model equations, parameters and methods

**What this section contains.** This section makes the model precise. We write it as a
constraint system in the style of Li et al. 2021 [@Li2021]: the enzyme-constrained flux problem
(@eq-ss–@eq-cap), then each temperature dependence in turn — the turnover $k_{\text{cat},i}(T)$
(@eq-kcat), the native (folded) fraction $f_{N,i}(T)$ (@eq-fn), and how the two combine into a
temperature-dependent per-flux enzyme cost (@eq-cost) — then our proteome-**sector** partition
(@eq-pool–@eq-alloc), the optional growth-law coupling (@eq-growthlaw), the temperature-dependent
maintenance $\text{NGAM}(T)$ (@eq-ngam), the FBA objective (@eq-obj), and the descriptors read
off the predicted curve. Every symbol is defined in prose; energies are in J mol⁻¹, entropies and
heat capacities in J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, temperatures in kelvin, $R=8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹. A reader who wants
the biology rather than the algebra can skip to the descriptor definitions and the provenance
table. We follow Li et al. for the thermal core and depart from it in structure (see *Relationship
to Li et al. 2021* below); the written equations mirror the implementation
(`enzyme_cost.py`, `unfolding.py`, `sectors.py`, `tpc.py`).

**1 — The enzyme-constrained model.** The model is an enzyme- and temperature-constrained
flux-balance model built on the **GECKO** enzyme-constrained reconstruction of iML1515
(**eciML1515**) [@Sanchez2017; @Domenzain2022; @Monk2017] for *E. coli* K-12 MG1655. Growth is the
biomass flux $v_\text{bio}$ found by linear programming under mass balance and enzyme capacity. At
steady state,

$$\mathbf{S}\,\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}, $$ {#eq-ss}

where $\mathbf{S}$ is the stoichiometric matrix and $\mathbf{v}$ the reaction-flux vector
(mmol gDW⁻¹ h⁻¹). Each reaction's flux is limited by the amount of catalytically competent
(**native**) enzyme,

$$0 \le v_i \le k_{\text{cat},i}(T)\,[E]_{N,i}(T),\qquad [E]_{N,i}(T)=f_{N,i}(T)\,[E]_i, $$ {#eq-cap}

with turnover number $k_{\text{cat},i}(T)$ (@eq-kcat), total enzyme $[E]_i$ and native fraction
$f_{N,i}(T)\in[0,1]$ (@eq-fn) — only folded protein catalyses, while denatured protein still occupies
the budget. Rather than carry an $[E]_i$ variable per reaction, we impose the mathematically
equivalent **sMOMENT** total-protein-pool form: a shared, sector-partitioned proteome budget bounds
the summed per-flux enzyme demand $\sum_i c_i(T)\,|v_i|$ (@eq-cost, @eq-pool). A temperature-dependent
non-growth maintenance demand $v_\text{ATPM}\ge\text{NGAM}(T)$ (@eq-maint) completes the system.

**2 — Temperature-dependent turnover $k_{\text{cat}}(T)$.** Each enzyme's turnover follows
transition-state (Eyring) theory with a macromolecular-rate-theory heat-capacity term,

$$k_{\text{cat},i}(T) = \frac{k_B T}{h}\,\exp\!\Big(\!-\frac{\Delta G^{\ddagger}_i(T)}{R\,T}\Big),\qquad
\Delta G^{\ddagger}_i(T) = \Delta H^{\ddagger}_i + \Delta C^{\ddagger}_{p,i}\,(T-T_0)
- T\big(\Delta S^{\ddagger}_i + \Delta C^{\ddagger}_{p,i}\ln(T/T_0)\big), $$ {#eq-kcat}

with Boltzmann constant $k_B$, Planck constant $h$, gas constant $R$, reference temperature $T_0$
(0 °C in the turnover code), and per-enzyme activation enthalpy, entropy and heat capacity
$\Delta H^{\ddagger}_i,\Delta S^{\ddagger}_i,\Delta C^{\ddagger}_{p,i}$. A negative
$\Delta C^{\ddagger}_p$ makes $\ln k_\text{cat}$ single-peaked in $T$. **Anchoring (a design
choice):** we carry turnover *relative to its value at each enzyme's optimum* $T_{\text{opt},i}$,

$$\widehat{k}_i(T) \equiv \frac{k_{\text{cat},i}(T)}{k_{\text{cat},i}(T_{\text{opt},i})},\qquad
\widehat{k}_i(T_{\text{opt},i})=1, $$

so $\widehat{k}$ is invariant to the absolute turnover level (that level is carried in $\text{base}_i$;
@eq-cost). $\Delta H^{\ddagger}_i$ is fixed by the peak condition $\mathrm{d}\ln k_\text{cat}/\mathrm{d}T=0$
at $T_{\text{opt},i}$ and is coupled, following the reference `etc.py`, to the unfolding thermodynamics
$(\Delta H_{TH,i},\Delta S_{TS,i},\Delta C_{p,u,i})$ of @eq-fn; $\Delta C^{\ddagger}_{p,i}$ is the
transition-state heat capacity.

**3 — Two-state unfolding / native fraction.** The high-temperature collapse is set by *denaturation*.
Each enzyme sits in a two-state native $\leftrightarrow$ unfolded equilibrium keyed on its melting
temperature $T_{m,i}$,

$$f_{N,i}(T) = \frac{1}{1+\exp\!\big(-\Delta G_{u,i}(T)/(R T)\big)},\qquad
\Delta G_{u,i}(T) = \Delta H_{TH,i} + \Delta C_{p,u,i}(T-T_H) - T\,\Delta S_{TS,i}
- T\,\Delta C_{p,u,i}\ln(T/T_S), $$ {#eq-fn}

with universal convergence temperatures $T_H=373.5$ K and $T_S=385$ K, and per-enzyme unfolding
enthalpy, entropy and heat capacity $(\Delta H_{TH,i},\Delta S_{TS,i},\Delta C_{p,u,i})$ solved from
$T_{m,i}$ together with $T_{90}$ (or protein length) by the conditions $\Delta G_{u,i}(T_{m,i})=0$ and
$\Delta G_{u,i}(T_{90})=-RT_{90}\ln 9$. $f_{N,i}$ falls from one to zero around $T_{m,i}$; because
denatured enzyme still consumes proteome but does not catalyse, the cost is inflated by $1/f_{N,i}$
(@eq-cost), which produces the high-temperature falling limb — keyed on protein *stability* $T_m$ and
so decoupled from the rising-limb activation energy.

**4 — Effective per-flux cost and the proteome-sector partition.** Combining the anchored turnover and
the native fraction, the per-flux enzyme cost is

$$c_i(T) = \frac{\text{base}_i}{\widehat{k}_i(T)\,f_{N,i}(T)\,\lambda_k},\qquad
\text{base}_i = \frac{\text{MW}_i}{k_{\text{cat},i}(T_{\text{opt},i})\cdot 3600}, $$ {#eq-cost}

where $\text{MW}_i$ is the enzyme molecular weight (kDa $=$ g mmol⁻¹), the factor $3600$ converts
$k_\text{cat}$ from s⁻¹ to h⁻¹, and $\lambda_k$ (`kcat_scale`, nominal $1$) is a global turnover-level
multiplier (part 8); $c_i$ has units g protein per (mmol gDW⁻¹ h⁻¹), so $\text{base}_i$ is the cost at
the enzyme's optimum. **Our upgrade over Li's single undivided pool** is to split the total modelled
protein $P_\text{tot}$ into coarse **proteome sectors** (Basan 2015; Scott 2010
[@Basan2015; @Scott2010]) summing to one — metabolic $f_\text{metab}$, biosynthesis/ribosomal
$f_\text{bio}$, and maintenance $f_\text{maint}$ (housekeeping $+$ chaperones), with
$f_\text{bio}=1-f_\text{metab}-f_\text{maint}$. Growth then maximises $v_\text{bio}$ under a
**metabolic-pool** bound, a **biosynthesis (translation) cap** and a **maintenance** floor:

$$\sum_i c_i(T)\,\big(v_i^{+}+v_i^{-}\big) \le f_\text{metab}\,P_\text{tot}, $$ {#eq-pool}

$$\kappa\,v_\text{bio} \le f_\text{bio}\,P_\text{tot}, $$ {#eq-biocap}

$$v_\text{ATPM} \ge \text{NGAM}(T)\,\frac{f_\text{maint}}{f_\text{maint}^{\text{nom}}}, $$ {#eq-maint}

where $v_i^{+},v_i^{-}\ge 0$ are the forward/reverse fluxes of reaction $i$, and $\kappa$
(`translation_coeff`) is the effective ribosomal protein demand per unit biomass flux. $\kappa$ is
auto-calibrated **once** at build time so that at the nominal split and $T_0$ the metabolic pool and
the translation cap are exactly co-limiting — so switching sectors on at the nominal point does not move
the nominal growth; it only opens the allocation axis. The emergent metabolic budget is

$$P_\text{metab} = f_\text{metab}\,P_\text{tot},\qquad P_\text{tot} = \sigma\,P^{\text{lit}}, $$ {#eq-alloc}

with $\sigma$ the average in-vivo enzyme saturation, $P^{\text{lit}}$ the literature total protein per
gram dry weight, and the sector fractions $(f_\text{metab},f_\text{bio},f_\text{maint})(m,T)$ taken from
the measured proteome, matched to growth medium $m$ and temperature $T$ — never fit to growth. This is
the mechanistic origin of the TPC: raising $T$ past an enzyme's $T_\text{opt}$ or $T_m$ inflates $c_i$,
tightens @eq-pool, and eventually starves growth.

**5 — Growth-law coupling (a config-selectable mode).** Optionally the sectors follow a
proteome-conserving bacterial growth law (Scott et al. 2010 [@Scott2010]): the ribosomal fraction rises
and the metabolic fraction falls with growth rate at the *same* slope $s$,
$f_\text{bio}(\mu)=f_{\text{bio},0}+s\,\mu$ and $f_\text{metab}(\mu)=f_{\text{metab},0}-s\,\mu$ with
$\mu=v_\text{bio}$, so both caps stay **linear** in $v_\text{bio}$ (no iteration):

$$(\kappa - s\,P_\text{tot})\,v_\text{bio} \le f_{\text{bio},0}\,P_\text{tot},\qquad
\sum_i c_i(T)\,\big(v_i^{+}+v_i^{-}\big) + s\,P_\text{tot}\,v_\text{bio} \le f_{\text{metab},0}\,P_\text{tot}, $$ {#eq-growthlaw}

with the $\mu=0$ ribosome floor $f_{\text{bio},0}$ grounded independently (Scott's $\phi_{R,\min}$) and
$f_{\text{metab},0}=1-f_\text{maint}-f_{\text{bio},0}$. As $\mu$ rises the shrinking metabolic sector
binds, so the **ribosome $\leftrightarrow$ metabolism trade-off**, not a static cap, sets the maximal
rate. This mode is off by default; the fixed measured partition (@eq-pool–@eq-maint) is the baseline.

**6 — Temperature-dependent maintenance $\text{NGAM}(T)$.** The non-growth-associated maintenance ATP
demand rises with temperature (ported from the MRes *E. coli* model; this departs from Li's yeast
maintenance form),

$$\text{NGAM}(T) = a_\text{m}\Big(1 - b\,\exp\!\big[\tfrac{-E_m}{k_B}\big(\tfrac{1}{T_\text{ref}}-\tfrac{1}{T}\big)\big]\Big),\quad\text{floored at its }T_\text{ref}\text{ value}, $$ {#eq-ngam}

with amplitude $a_\text{m}\approx 8.5$ mmol ATP gDW⁻¹ h⁻¹, $b\approx 0.62$, apparent activation energy
$E_m\approx 0.5$ eV, reference $T_\text{ref}=298.15$ K (25 °C) and $k_B=8.617\times10^{-5}$ eV K⁻¹. Below
$T_\text{ref}$ the demand is held at its 25 °C floor. In sector mode the constraint is scaled by the
maintenance-sector ratio $f_\text{maint}/f_\text{maint}^{\text{nom}}$ (@eq-maint) and, temperature-wise,
by $\text{NGAM}(T)/\text{NGAM}(T_\text{ref})$. The amplitude and steepness carry the
`ngam_scale`/`ngam_steepness` levers (part 8).

**7 — Objective and curve descriptors.** At each grid temperature FBA maximises the biomass flux
subject to mass balance (@eq-ss), the metabolic pool (@eq-pool), the biosynthesis cap (@eq-biocap)
and the maintenance floor (@eq-maint),

$$\mu(T) = \max_{\mathbf v}\ v_\text{bio}, $$ {#eq-obj}

tracing the organismal TPC $\mu(T)$. From it we read a standard set of **thermal-performance-curve
descriptors**: the **optimum** $T_\text{opt}=\arg\max_T\mu$; the **maximum growth rate**
$r_\text{max}=\max_T\mu$ (h⁻¹); the **critical thermal limits** $CT_\text{min}$/$CT_\text{max}$, the cold
and hot temperatures where $\mu$ falls to a critical fraction (5 %) of $r_\text{max}$, by linear
interpolation of the crossing; the **niche width** $CT_\text{max}-CT_\text{min}$; the $80\%$ breadth
$B_{80}$ (the width over which $\mu\ge0.8\,r_\text{max}$); the rising-limb **activation energy** $E_a$,
the slope of $\ln\mu$ against $-1/(k_B T)$ over the rising portion ($0.1\,r_\text{max}<\mu<0.95\,r_\text{max}$;
the Boltzmann–Arrhenius measure, in eV); and the curve **skewness**.

**8 — Global correction / perturbation parameters.** The sensitivity, decomposition and Bayesian
analyses move the model through a small set of **global scalars** that transform the *whole* grounded
per-enzyme distribution while preserving its shape — they are not per-enzyme free parameters
(@tbl-knobs). Each keeps every enzyme's grounded value relative to the rest and only slides, stretches
or rescales the population.

| Knob | Transformation | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| $dT_\text{opt}$ | $T_{\text{opt},i}\to T_{\text{opt},i}+dT_\text{opt}$ | uniform shift of every optimum |
| `topt_scale` | $T_{\text{opt},i}\to T_0+\texttt{topt\_scale}\,(T_{\text{opt},i}-T_0)$ | spread of optima about $T_0$ |
| `dCp_scale` | $\Delta C^{\ddagger}_{p,i}\to\texttt{dCp\_scale}\,\Delta C^{\ddagger}_{p,i}$ | turnover curvature / breadth |
| $dT_m$ | evaluate $f_{N,i}$ at $T-dT_m$ | uniform melting shift ($+dT_m$ raises $CT_\text{max}$) |
| `tm_scale` | $T_{m,i}\to \bar T_m+\texttt{tm\_scale}\,(T_{m,i}-\bar T_m)$ | spread of melting temperatures |
| `kcat_scale` ($\lambda_k$) | $c_i\to c_i/\lambda_k$ | global turnover *level* → $r_\text{max}$ (leaves $E_a,T_\text{opt},CT_\text{max}$) |
| `kappa_scale` | @eq-biocap RHS $\times\,\texttt{kappa\_scale}$ ($\equiv\kappa/\texttt{kappa\_scale}$) | translation efficiency → $r_\text{max}$ |
| $f_\text{metab},f_\text{maint}$ | set the sector simplex ($f_\text{bio}=1-f_\text{metab}-f_\text{maint}$) | proteome allocation |
| `ngam_scale`, `ngam_steepness` | scale $a_\text{m}$ and $E_m$ in @eq-ngam | maintenance amplitude / T-steepness |

: Global correction knobs — each is one scalar acting on the whole per-enzyme distribution, with its defining transformation. All are no-ops at their nominal (0 for additive shifts, 1 for multipliers). {#tbl-knobs}

**9 — Parameter provenance.** Every input is grounded in independent data or a stated literature value;
none is fit to the growth curve in the emergent model (@tbl-provenance).

| Symbol | Quantity | Source | Coverage / value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{MW}_i, k_{\text{cat,ref},i}$ | enzyme mass, reference turnover | GECKO ecModel of iML1515 [@Sanchez2017; @Domenzain2022; @Monk2017] | 2560 enzymes |
| $T_{\text{opt},i}$ | catalytic optimum | Li–Engqvist sequence predictor [@LiEngqvist2019]; DLTKcat overlay [@Qiu2024] | 90 % grounded; 13 DLTKcat |
| $T_{m,i}$ | melting temperature | *E. coli* melting proteome / Meltome [@Leuenberger2017] | 90 % grounded, rest at mean 55.6 °C |
| $\Delta C^{\ddagger}_{p,i}$ | turnover curvature | literature MMRT prior −4 kJ mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ [@Hobbs2013] + DLTKcat | prior for all; 13 DLTKcat |
| $T_H, T_S$ | unfolding convergence temps | universal protein-folding constants [@Li2021] | 373.5 K / 385 K |
| $f_\text{metab},f_\text{bio},f_\text{maint}$ | sector fractions | measured proteome, per medium & $T$ [@Wang2026] | LB/Glucose/Glycerol, 16–43 °C |
| $P^{\text{lit}}$ | total protein per gDW | literature | 0.5 g/gDW |
| $\sigma$ | in-vivo enzyme saturation | Davidi 2016 / Heckmann 2020 [@Davidi2016; @Heckmann2020] | 0.45 (range 0.4–0.5) |
| $s$ | growth-law slope | Scott et al. 2010 ($\sim1/\kappa_t$) [@Scott2010] | 0.33 (h) — coupled mode only |
| $a_\text{m}, b, E_m, T_\text{ref}$ | NGAM$(T)$ constants | MRes *E. coli* maintenance port [@Madkaikar2023] | 8.5 / 0.62 / 0.5 eV / 298 K |
| validation curve | exact-strain TPC | K-12 MG1655 in BHI, 7–46 °C [@VanDerlinden2012]; rich medium def. from MRes/Machado [@Madkaikar2023; @Machado2021] | 1 curve (+1 cross-check) |

: Parameter provenance — every value grounded in independent data or a stated literature value, never fit to growth. {#tbl-provenance}

**Model hierarchy — how the components link.** The model is a hierarchy that turns grounded
per-enzyme thermal parameters into an organismal curve, each level feeding only the next.
**(i)** Every enzyme carries three grounded thermal parameters — its optimum $T_{\text{opt},i}$,
its melting temperature $T_{m,i}$ and its turnover curvature $\Delta C^{\ddagger}_{p,i}$.
**(ii)** These set the two per-enzyme temperature responses: the optimum-anchored turnover
$\widehat{k}_i(T)$ (@eq-kcat) and the native (folded) fraction $f_{N,i}(T)$ (@eq-fn).
**(iii)** The two combine into an effective per-flux enzyme cost $c_i(T)$ (@eq-cost).
**(iv)** The costs enter the sector-partitioned proteome constraints — metabolic pool,
biosynthesis cap and temperature-dependent maintenance (@eq-pool–@eq-maint) — optionally coupled
through the growth law (@eq-growthlaw). **(v)** FBA maximises biomass flux at each temperature
(@eq-obj), tracing the TPC $\mu(T)$. **(vi)** The curve is reduced to descriptors, and
**(vii)** for the analyses, the global correction knobs (@tbl-knobs) deform the whole per-enzyme
distribution at level (i). So the forward chain is *grounded parameters $\to$ per-enzyme responses
$\to$ cost $\to$ sector constraints $+$ growth law $+$ $\text{NGAM}(T)$ $\to$ FBA growth $\to$
descriptors*, with the knobs acting at the parameter level and no growth-curve information entering
anywhere in the emergent model.

**Relationship to, and departures from, the Li et al. 2021 yeast etc-GEM.** Our model is a direct
descendant of the yeast etc-GEM of Li et al. 2021 (Nat. Commun. 12:190) [@Li2021], and we **follow
it for the thermal core**: the transition-state turnover $k_{\text{cat}}(T)$ (@eq-kcat), the
two-state native-fraction denaturation keyed on $T_m$ (@eq-fn), a temperature-dependent maintenance
term, and the enzyme-constrained proteome-pool idea. We **depart from / upgrade** it in five
deliberate ways, each with a rationale:

- **Organism.** *E. coli* iML1515 / K-12 MG1655 (eciML1515) rather than their *S. cerevisiae*
  ecYeast7.6 — the best-curated bacterial model, chosen as a proving ground before scaling to a
  phylogenetically diverse isolate library.
- **Grounded, not fitted, per-enzyme parameters.** We fix each enzyme's $T_\text{opt}$, $T_m$ and
  curvature from independent data (a sequence-based optimum predictor, the measured melting proteome,
  an MMRT prior refined by DLTKcat), so the TPC is an **a-priori forward prediction**. Li et al.
  instead infer the per-enzyme thermal parameters by fitting the growth data — the opposite
  epistemic stance. Grounding is what lets us *test* the emergent $E_a$ and magnitude against an
  independent benchmark rather than recover values we tuned.
- **Sector-partitioned proteome, not a single pool.** We replace the single undivided protein pool
  with a **measured, medium- and temperature-matched proteome-sector partition** (metabolic /
  biosynthesis / maintenance; @eq-pool–@eq-maint) plus an explicit biosynthesis/translation cap
  (@eq-biocap). This encodes the bacterial growth laws directly and makes proteome allocation an
  attributable, measurable control on the rate — at a modest cost in raw growth-curve fit.
- **Growth-rate-dependent allocation.** An optional proteome-conserving growth law (@eq-growthlaw)
  lets the ribosome $\leftrightarrow$ metabolism trade-off, rather than a static cap, set the maximal
  rate — closer to the physiology of the Scott/Basan laws.
- **Inference by likelihood, not ABC.** We calibrate a **low-dimensional set of global correction
  knobs** (@tbl-knobs) by likelihood-based ensemble MCMC (emcee), kept as a **separate** inverse
  analysis layered on top of the emergent prior — contrasting with Li et al.'s SMC-ABC over the
  full per-enzyme parameter set. An exact likelihood is available because the etc-GEM is a
  deterministic simulator with a Gaussian residual model; keeping the emergent prediction and the
  tuned inverse problem separate is precisely what lets us report an honest a-priori gap instead of a
  fit.

**In-silico experimental design.** Every analysis reuses the same deterministic
`compute_tpc`/`descriptors` engine and moves the model *only* through the global correction knobs
(@tbl-knobs); none refits the per-enzyme parameters. Five analyses build on one another.

- **(O1) Emergent a-priori prediction.** The nominal model — with **nothing fit to growth** —
  predicts the reference curve (@eq-obj) and its descriptors on the 5–60 °C grid. This prediction is
  the prior for everything downstream and is validated a priori against the exact-strain curve
  (*Validation* below).
- **(O2/O3) Equal-perturbation elasticity.** Each knob is nudged by the *same* standardised step
  $\pm h$ ($h=0.10$) by central finite difference and we record the fractional change it produces in
  each descriptor (@eq-elasticity) — a **local, magnitude-based** sensitivity in which every input is
  directly comparable because it is perturbed by the same relative amount (the additive shifts
  $dT_\text{opt}$/$dT_m$ move by $\pm h\,\Delta_\text{ref}$, $\Delta_\text{ref}=$ the per-enzyme
  $T_\text{opt}$/$T_m$ SD $=4.25$/$6.13$ K).
- **(O4) Allocation / kinetic / stability decomposition.** A range-fair, nominal-centred,
  fully-crossed **three-way** (allocation × kinetic-envelope × stability) functional ANOVA, every
  parameter moved by the same standardised half-width $H=0.2$ about its measured value (allocation
  $=$ the sector split $f_\text{metab}$/$f_\text{maint}$, which carries maintenance; kinetic $=$
  $dT_\text{opt}$ / spread / curvature; stability $=$ $dT_m$). It yields both the two-group Shapley
  split $\varphi_A=S_A+\tfrac12 S_{AE}$, $\varphi_E=S_E+\tfrac12 S_{AE}$ (allocation vs whole
  envelope) and the three-group allocation/kinetic/stability shares, each reported alongside an
  achievable-IQR magnitude and the family of reachable growth curves per lever.
- **(O2/O5) Per-enzyme control and identifiability.** We screen all 2560 enzymes by a thermal-control
  score (proteome usage × thermal sensitivity), compute central-finite-difference control
  coefficients of each descriptor to each top-enzyme's $T_{\text{opt},i}$/$\Delta C^{\ddagger}_{p,i}$,
  and report proteome-wide identifiability — the max-normalised control magnitude, a first-order
  proxy (not Fisher information) — over all $2560\times3$ per-enzyme parameters.
- **(O6) Bayesian calibration (emergent vs tuned).** Treating the emergent prediction as the
  **prior**, we ask the inverse question by exact likelihood-based inference with a gradient-free
  ensemble sampler (**emcee**): because the etc-GEM is a deterministic simulator with a Gaussian
  residual model, an exact likelihood is available (it is **not** ABC). The free set is the
  low-dimensional, elasticity-selected group of global knobs
  $\{dT_\text{opt},\,dT_m,\,\texttt{dCp\_scale},\,\texttt{kappa\_scale}\}$ plus a nuisance noise
  scale, with the saturation $\sigma$ and the measured sector fractions held **fixed**; priors are
  centred on the emergent values (0, 0, 1, 1) with provenance-based widths
  ($dT_\text{opt}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,4.25\,\text{K})$, $dT_m\sim\mathcal{N}(0,6.13\,\text{K})$,
  `dCp_scale`/`kappa_scale` LogNormal about 1), positive parameters sampled in log space. Keeping the
  emergent prior and the tuned posterior **separate** quantifies how far the borrowed/uncertain
  quantities must move to reproduce a curve, and how well one curve constrains them.

**Validation.** The model is validated against the one curve measured on the exact GEM strain —
K-12 MG1655 in BHI broth over 7–46 °C (Van Derlinden 2012 [@VanDerlinden2012], figure-digitized)
— predicted a priori under a rich medium (availability, uptake unpinned) with its medium-matched
sector fractions. The **primary metric is absolute** $R^2$/RMSE on raw specific growth rate
($\text{h}^{-1}$); the rising-limb $E_a$ (tested against the ~0.85 eV mesophilic-growth
benchmark) is a secondary diagnostic. The proteome is validated separately (predicted vs
measured per-enzyme mass; see the Supplementary Information).

# Validation against the exact-strain rich curve (Van Derlinden, MG1655)

**What this section does.** The strongest test of a forward model is whether it reproduces
real data it never saw. We validate the model — **with nothing fit to growth** — against the
one trusted growth curve measured on the *exact* strain the genome-scale model is built from:
**Van Derlinden & Van Impe 2012** [@VanDerlinden2012], *E. coli* K-12 MG1655 (CGSC #6300) in
Brain-Heart-Infusion (BHI) broth, spanning 7–46 °C with a peak near 2.4 h⁻¹. Its wide span
covers the cold rising limb and the hot collapse. The model is predicted a priori under a rich
medium and scored on **raw absolute growth rate (h⁻¹)**.

**Honest caveats.** Van Derlinden is **figure-digitized** (no per-temperature SD); and a rich
medium is a **less stringent test than a defined minimal medium**, because supplying amino acids
and nucleosides relaxes the biosynthetic enzyme demand the model constrains most tightly. An
MG1655 *defined-minimal* TPC remains the gold-standard future test.

**Medium as an input (BHI by availability).** The medium enters as *availability*, not as a
pinned uptake rate: we open the exchange uptakes (`EX_*_REV`) of the medium's components and let
the enzyme-constrained model determine actual uptake (pinning uptake rates would be a plain-FBA
workaround; here the enzyme constraints cap growth). BHI is not encoded *in silico* in the
literature — even a recent *Rothia* genome-scale study used LB/TSB as defined rich stand-ins and
treated BHI only as an in-vitro baseline [@RothiaGEM2024] — so we approximate BHI by a **curated rich
component list** (our LB list plus tissue-extract extras, glucose included, plant-sugar TSB
components excluded, asparagine/glutamine omitted by the hydrolysate convention); 72 of 76
components map to the model. Because the measured proteome (LB/glucose/glycerol) has no BHI
entry, BHI uses the **LB (rich) medium-matched sector allocation**.

The rich operating point is the model **configured exactly as the later calibration tunes it**:
the reconciled single proteome pool (Methods), the coupled biosynthesis–metabolic growth law
engaged, and temperature-dependent maintenance restored (the latter two rounding the peak so it
plateaus rather than spiking at a single temperature).

**Result — the model tracks the shape but under-predicts the magnitude ~2.3-fold.** Predicted
a priori under BHI (@fig-valcurves, @tbl-val), the emergent model reproduces the *shape* of the
Van Derlinden curve — the cold rising limb, a **rounded optimum at** $T_\text{opt}=40$ °C
(observed 40 °C), a rising-limb $E_a=0.63$ eV (observed 0.64 eV; both a touch below the ~0.85 eV
mesophilic-growth benchmark, and matching each other closely), and a hot-side collapse
($\text{CT}_\text{max}\approx51$ °C vs observed 46 °C) — giving a positive absolute $R^2=0.27$
over the full 7–46 °C span. What it does not capture is the **absolute level**: the predicted
peak $r_\text{max}=1.04$ h⁻¹ is about **2.3-fold below** the observed 2.40 h⁻¹. This is an honest
a-priori gap, not a tuning failure (nothing is fit): a rich medium relaxes the enzyme constraints
so the biosynthesis cap and pool saturate, and the shortfall points to the in-vivo $k_\text{cat}$
scale, the saturation $\sigma$, or the exact medium composition — the levers the Calibration
section (next) examines.

![**Emergent model vs the exact-strain curve (raw absolute rate, nothing fit).** Van Derlinden 2012, K-12 MG1655 in BHI, 7–46 °C (figure-digitized), predicted a priori under the rich medium. The model line tracks the shape but sits ~2.3-fold below the peak; absolute $R^2$/RMSE and observed-vs-predicted $r_\text{max}$/$T_\text{opt}$ are annotated.](assets/figures/validation_trusted_curves.png){#fig-valcurves fig-pos="H" width=70%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-val
#| tbl-cap: "Validation against Van Derlinden (K-12 MG1655, BHI). Nothing is fit to growth: the model tracks the shape (Topt, Ea) but under-predicts the absolute peak ~2.3-fold under the rich medium."
#| output: asis
import json, pandas as pd
with open("assets/tables/validation_trusted_summary.json") as fh:
    s = json.load(fh)
pr = s["vanderlinden_bhi"]
rows = [("strain", "K-12 MG1655"),
        ("medium", "BHI (rich)"),
        ("T range (°C)", f"{pr['T_range_C'][0]:.0f}–{pr['T_range_C'][1]:.0f}"),
        ("n temperatures", pr["n"]),
        ("absolute R²", pr["abs_R2"]),
        ("RMSE (h⁻¹)", pr["RMSE_per_h"]),
        ("observed r_max (h⁻¹)", pr["obs_rmax"]),
        ("predicted r_max (h⁻¹)", pr["pred_rmax"]),
        ("observed T_opt (°C)", pr["obs_Topt_C"]),
        ("predicted T_opt (°C)", pr["pred_Topt_C"]),
        ("observed E_a (eV)", pr["obs_Ea_eV"]),
        ("predicted E_a (eV)", pr["pred_Ea_eV"])]
print(pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=["quantity", "Van Derlinden (MG1655, BHI)"]).to_markdown(index=False))
```

# Calibration: what the data demand {#sec-calibration}

**What this section does.** The Validation section showed the emergent model reproduces the
*shape* of the exact-strain curve a priori but under-predicts the absolute rich peak. Here we ask
the complementary, explicitly-labelled **inverse** question: treating the emergent prediction as a
prior, *what corrections does the measured curve demand* of the few borrowed or uncertain **global**
knobs — and how much of the a-priori gap do they close? This is a Bayesian inverse analysis
(gradient-free ensemble MCMC, exact Gaussian discrepancy likelihood on raw absolute rate) fit to the
single Van Derlinden curve at the rich (BHI) operating point, with the coupled growth law engaged and
the reconciled single proteome pool (below). The grounded per-enzyme parameters are **not** re-fit;
only the unified set of global correction levers is (@tbl-knobs), each with a provenance-based prior.
Nothing about the emergent prediction is relabelled — the point is to quantify the gap, not paper
over it.

**The free set and priors.** Twelve global levers plus a free discrepancy term: the
envelope/stability shape {$dT_\text{opt}$, `topt_scale`, `dCp_scale`, $dT_m$, `tm_scale`}; the
magnitude/catalysis levers {`kcat_scale`, `kappa_scale`, and the in-vivo saturation $\sigma$}; the
measured allocation {$f_\text{metab}$, $f_\text{maint}$}; and the borrowed maintenance
{`ngam_scale`, `ngam_steepness`}. Measured sector fractions get **tight** priors (measurement wiggle
only — the proteome is not free-fit); the magnitude levers get their physical priors — `kcat_scale`
a broad LogNormal about 1 (the in-vitro→in-vivo turnover gap is real and several-fold), and $\sigma$
a broad Normal on the literature ~0.45 **bounded to the physical $(0,1]$**. With the proteome pools
reconciled, $\sigma$ is the effective budget lever: `kcat_scale` relaxes only the metabolic per-flux
cost and saturates once the ribosome cap binds, whereas $\sigma$ scales the whole enzyme-mass budget
and lifts **both** caps. The discrepancy term $\sigma_\text{disc}$ is free because the figure-
digitized Van Derlinden curve carries no measured SD.

**Result — the magnitude gap is mostly closed, and the shape holds** (@fig-postcurve,
@tbl-corrections). The posterior-predictive peak rises from the emergent $r_\text{max}\approx1.04$ to
**2.14 h⁻¹ [1.98, 2.29]** — about **89 % of the way** to the observed 2.40 h⁻¹ — while the shape
stays put: the rounded optimum is $T_\text{opt}=38$ °C (observed 40), and the hot collapse is pulled
in from the emergent ~52 °C to $CT_\text{max}=45.9$ °C (matching the observed 46) by a melting-
temperature shift $dT_m=-5.6$ K $[-7.2,-2.8]$. The rising-limb $E_a$ sits at 0.92 eV, near the
~0.85 eV bacterial-growth benchmark; the free discrepancy term ($\sigma_\text{disc}\approx0.20$ h⁻¹)
absorbs the possibly-artefactually-low figure-digitized observed $E_a$ (0.64) rather than the fit
chasing it. The measured sectors barely move ($f_\text{metab}=0.281$, $f_\text{maint}=0.341$, at their
measured priors), and `kappa_scale`/`ngam_*`/`topt_scale`/`dCp_scale`/`tm_scale` stay near their
priors — one curve weakly constrains them.

**What the tuning reveals about each feature of the curve** (@tbl-corrections, @fig-corner). The
demanded corrections are a *second, independent* line of evidence on what drives each TPC feature —
not the structural decomposition of the later sections, but a direct reading of what the data
actually had to **correct** in the a-priori model. Feature by feature:

- **Magnitude ($r_\text{max}$ $1.04\!\to\!2.14$ vs observed 2.40).** The data demanded **catalytic
  capacity and nothing in the envelope**: the in-vivo saturation $\sigma$ railed to its 1.0 ceiling
  ($0.867$, above the 0.4–0.5 literature range) with a modest turnover bump (`kcat_scale` 1.25), on
  an anti-correlated ridge (both relax the metabolic pool, but only $\sigma$ *also* lifts the
  ribosome cap, so $\sigma$ is the lever that reaches the peak). The height is a **capacity**
  quantity, and the a-priori shortfall was purely capacity, not shape.
- **Upper limit ($CT_\text{max}$ $51.8\!\to\!45.9$ vs 45.9).** The data demanded a melting-temperature
  shift $dT_m=-5.6$ K and essentially nothing else — the emergent melting proteome sat a few K too
  hot, and the tuning corrected only that **stability** axis. The hot collapse is set by $T_m$.
- **Optimum ($T_\text{opt}$ $39.9\!\to\!38.4$ vs 40).** $dT_\text{opt}$ and `topt_scale` barely
  moved (the $dT_\text{opt}$ 90 % CI touches zero; `topt_scale`$\approx0.96$): the sequence/kinetic-
  grounded optimum was **already right a priori** and needed no material correction.
- **Rising limb / $E_a$.** $dC_p$ was only weakly constrained and the free discrepancy term absorbed
  the possibly-flattened figure-digitized observed $E_a$ (0.64) rather than the fit chasing it — so
  the cold-side shape stays **envelope/kinetic**, and a single curve does not pin it (the posterior
  $E_a\approx0.92$ tracks the ~0.85 eV benchmark, not the digitized 0.64).

**The headline.** The grounded, sequence- and structure-based envelope got the **shape and position
of the TPC essentially right out of the box** — the optimum, the cold-side activation energy and the
rounded peak — and the data only had to correct the **capacity** (magnitude) and fine-tune the
**upper limit** ($T_m$). That is a strong statement that the a-priori envelope is trustworthy and
that growth data mainly inform the catalytic budget. It **agrees with the tuned-model decomposition**
below (magnitude owns the height; stability owns $T_\text{opt}$/$CT_\text{max}$; kinetics owns the
cold side): two independent arguments — one from what the data had to *correct*, one from the
structural variance *shares* — reach the same division of labour. The one honest caveat, carried to
the Interpretation, is that $\sigma$ pinned to its bound and the growth-law $f_\text{metab}$ squeeze
make the residual $2.14\!\to\!2.40$ a tight-budget frontier, not a fully closed gap. The **tuned
model** defined by these posterior medians — rich BHI, growth law on, reconciled pool — is the
baseline that the sensitivity, decomposition and identifiability sections below dissect.

![**Prior vs posterior on raw absolute rate (Van Derlinden, MG1655/BHI).** Emergent prior (dashed), posterior-predictive median and 90 % band (orange), and the digitized data. Calibration lifts the peak ~89 % of the way to the observed value and pulls the hot collapse in to match, while the rising limb is unchanged.](assets/figures/prior_vs_posterior_tpc.png){#fig-postcurve fig-pos="H" width=78%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-corrections
#| tbl-cap: "Demanded corrections (P2 v3 posterior): per global lever, the emergent value, the posterior median [90% CI], and whether the single curve constrains it. Magnitude is carried by sigma (railed toward its 1.0 ceiling) with a modest kcat_scale; the hot side by dTm; measured sectors barely move."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd
c = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/demanded_corrections.csv")
order = ["kcat_scale", "kappa_scale", "sigma", "dTm", "dTopt", "dCp_scale", "topt_scale",
         "tm_scale", "f_metab", "f_maint", "ngam_scale", "ngam_steepness", "sigma_disc"]
c["__o"] = c["parameter"].apply(lambda p: order.index(p) if p in order else 99)
c = c.sort_values("__o")
c["90% CI"] = c.apply(lambda r: f"[{r['ci5']:.3g}, {r['ci95']:.3g}]", axis=1)
c["posterior median"] = c["posterior_median"].round(3)
c["emergent"] = c["emergent"].round(3)
show = c[["parameter", "emergent", "posterior median", "90% CI", "demanded_correction",
          "constrained_by_curve"]].rename(columns={"demanded_correction": "correction",
          "constrained_by_curve": "constrained"})
print(show.to_markdown(index=False))
```

![**Posterior degeneracy (corner).** The full 13-parameter posterior. The magnitude ridge is the anti-correlated `kcat_scale`–$\sigma$ pair; $\sigma$ piles against its 1.0 bound; $dT_m$ is cleanly pulled negative; the rest sit near their priors.](assets/figures/corner_v3.png){#fig-corner fig-pos="H" width=98%}

# Which parameters move the curve? (local sensitivity) {#sec-sensitivity}

**What this section does.** With the model now tuned to the exact-strain curve (Calibration), we
ask which of its inputs actually control each feature of the growth curve, **around the tuned
operating point** (rich BHI, growth law on, reconciled single pool). The cleanest way to compare
inputs on an equal footing is an **elasticity**: nudge one input by a small, standardised amount and
measure the fractional change it produces in a curve feature. Because every input is nudged by the
*same* relative amount, the numbers are directly comparable — a larger elasticity is a stronger
lever. This is a *local* measure (it describes the model right at its operating point); the next
section gives the complementary *global* view.

**Method.** Around the **tuned** model we perturb each input by the same standardised step $\pm h$
(here $h=0.10$) by central finite difference and compute, for each descriptor $D$, a local
**elasticity**

$$E[D,p] = \frac{D(p+)-D(p-)}{2\,h\,D_\text{tuned}}, $$ {#eq-elasticity}

the fractional change in $D$ per standardised step of input $p$. Multiplicative inputs
(`topt_scale`, `dCp_scale`, `tm_scale`, `kcat_scale`, `kappa_scale`, $\sigma$, the sector fractions,
`ngam_scale`, `ngam_steepness`) move to $\text{base}\times(1\pm h)$; the additive shifts
$dT_\text{opt}$ and $dT_m$ move by $\pm h\,\Delta_\text{ref}$ with $\Delta_\text{ref}=$ the standard
deviation of the per-enzyme $T_\text{opt}$ / $T_m$ distribution, so each is "$h$ of a natural
temperature scale". The **input set is exactly the unified set the calibration used** —
envelope/stability {$dT_\text{opt}$, `topt_scale`, `dCp_scale`, $dT_m$, `tm_scale`},
magnitude/catalysis {`kcat_scale`, `kappa_scale`, $\sigma$}, allocation {$f_\text{metab}$,
$f_\text{maint}$} and maintenance {`ngam_scale`, `ngam_steepness`} — so the levers scored here are
precisely the ones the data constrained. We **propagate the posterior**: each elasticity is
recomputed over 150 posterior draws, giving a 90 % band on every elasticity (the whiskers in
@fig-elast-tornado). Because every input uses the same $h$, the elasticities are directly comparable
in magnitude (@fig-elast-tornado, @fig-elast-heat, @tbl-elasticity).

**The inputs are global dials on whole distributions, not per-enzyme knobs.** This is the key
thing to understand about what these analyses do. Each input is a single **global scalar** that
moves the *whole* per-enzyme parameter distribution (shown in the Supplementary Information) while preserving its shape:
$dT_\text{opt}$ slides every enzyme's optimum up or down together (a rigid shift of the
$T_\text{opt}$ density), `topt_scale` stretches or compresses the spread of optima about their
mean, $dT_m$ slides the entire melting-temperature density, `dCp_scale` scales every enzyme's
curvature, `tm_scale` scales the spread of melting temperatures, `kcat_scale` and $\sigma$ set the
catalytic magnitude, and $f_\text{metab}$/$f_\text{maint}$ set whole-cell allocation. No enzyme is
given a free parameter of its own — each keeps its grounded value, and the dial only moves the
population. So these twelve dials are a deliberately **low-dimensional summary of the
~7,700-parameter per-enzyme space** (2,560 enzymes × 3 thermal parameters): the heterogeneity
(illustrated in the Supplementary Information) is preserved throughout, but not individually tuned.
(The per-enzyme *control* analysis later does act enzyme-by-enzyme, and is the complementary
fine-grained view.)

**Result — magnitude sets the rate; stability sets the optimum and the hot limit; kinetics set the
cold side and $E_a$.** With the proteome pools reconciled, the picture is clean:

- **$r_\text{max}$ is driven by the magnitude levers.** The dominant lever is the in-vivo
  saturation $\sigma$ ($E=+1.0$ — a 10 % rise in saturation buys ~10 % more growth), with the
  metabolic turnover `kcat_scale` ($+0.40$) and, negatively, the maintenance sector $f_\text{maint}$
  ($-0.56$); the envelope barely moves it. **This is a direct consequence of the pool
  reconciliation:** before the two redundant enzyme-mass pools were merged, the magnitude levers
  pushed against a pool with slack and had $\approx0$ elasticity on $r_\text{max}$; on the single
  binding pool they genuinely move the rate. `kappa_scale` and $f_\text{metab}$ are inert here (the
  growth-law biosynthesis cap is not the binding constraint at the peak, and $f_\text{metab}$ is set
  by the $\mu$-coupled law).
- **$CT_\text{max}$ has one lever family — protein stability.** It responds only to the melting-
  temperature axis (`tm_scale` $-0.16$, $dT_m$ $+0.13$) and is insensitive to magnitude, allocation
  and kinetics: the hot limit is set by protein stability alone.
- **$T_\text{opt}$ is stability-led** at the tuned rounded peak (`tm_scale` $-0.26$, $dT_m$ $+0.13$):
  the optimum sits where the temperature-dependent maintenance and unfolding collapse begin.
- **$E_a$ and thermal breadth are kinetic-led:** the spread of enzyme optima `topt_scale` dominates
  both $E_a$ ($+1.4$) and the niche width ($-1.1$), with `dCp_scale` and the magnitude levers
  secondary.

The equal-step *local* elasticities isolate the structural leverage — magnitude (catalysis) for the
rate, stability/$T_m$ for the optimum and the upper limit, and the kinetic envelope for the cold
side and $E_a$. The posterior bands (whiskers) show these rankings are robust across the calibrated
uncertainty. The next section confirms the same division of labour with a *global*, grouped variance
decomposition and the interaction made explicit.

![**Equal-perturbation elasticities on the tuned model — $r_\text{max}$.** Levers ranked by $|E|$ (bars = posterior median, whiskers = 90 % CI over posterior draws; coloured by group). At the rich peak $r_\text{max}$ is magnitude-led — the in-vivo saturation $\sigma$ ($E\approx1$) and `kcat_scale`, with the maintenance sector opposing — a direct consequence of the pool reconciliation.](assets/figures/elasticity_tornado_rmax.png){#fig-elast-tornado fig-pos="H" width=80%}

![**Elasticity heatmap** (unified levers × descriptors, tuned model, standardised step $h=0.10$). $r_\text{max}$ is owned by the catalysis row ($\sigma$/`kcat_scale`); $CT_\text{max}$/$T_\text{opt}$ by the stability rows ($dT_m$/`tm_scale`); $E_a$/niche width by `topt_scale`.](assets/figures/elasticity_heatmap.png){#fig-elast-heat fig-pos="H" width=80%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-elasticity
#| tbl-cap: "Standardised elasticities E[D,p] on the TUNED model at the rich BHI operating point (equal step h=0.10; additive dTopt/dTm by ±h·Δref = h·sd of enzyme Topt/Tm; posterior median). Directly comparable in magnitude across the unified lever set. r_max is magnitude/catalysis-led; the shape descriptors are envelope-led."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

e = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/elasticity_table.csv", index_col=0)
keep = [c for c in ["Topt_C", "rmax", "CTmax_C", "niche_width_C", "Ea_eV"] if c in e.columns]
print(e[keep].round(2).to_markdown())
```

# What sets each feature of the curve: envelope or magnitude? (objective O4)

**What this section does.** The elasticity above measured how much each individual lever moves each
curve feature, locally. Here we group the twelve levers into the two things a thermal model can
change and ask how much of each feature's variability each group owns, **on the tuned model at the
rich operating point**. The two groups are the **envelope** — the temperature-dependence: where
enzymes work best, how curved their turnover is, and where they unfold ({$dT_\text{opt}$,
`topt_scale`, `dCp_scale`, $dT_m$, `tm_scale`}) — and the **magnitude** — how much catalytic
capacity the cell can bring to bear: allocation {$f_\text{metab}$, $f_\text{maint}$} $+$ catalysis
{`kcat_scale`, `kappa_scale`, $\sigma$} $+$ maintenance {`ngam_scale`, `ngam_steepness`}. Objective
O4 asks whether these divide the labour cleanly: does magnitude set the curve's *height* while the
envelope sets its *shape and position*? We also report the finer five-group split (kinetic /
stability / allocation / catalysis / maintenance) through the achievable magnitude of each sub-group.

**How the decomposition works, in words.** We vary the two groups together over a fully-crossed grid
and record each curve feature across the ensemble. A **variance decomposition** splits the total
spread of each feature into the part attributable to each group alone plus an **interaction** part
— variability that appears only when the two move together. Each group's share is a **Shapley
value** ($\varphi$), a fair-division rule that splits the interaction evenly, so the shares sum to
one. Every lever is moved by the same standardised relative half-width $H=0.2$ about its **tuned**
value (multiplicative levers over $\text{value}\times(1\pm H)$; the additive shifts $dT_\text{opt}$
and $dT_m$ over $\pm H\,\Delta_\text{ref}$, $\Delta_\text{ref}=$ the sd of the per-enzyme
$T_\text{opt}$ / $T_m$ distribution), so the shares reflect the model's structure rather than how
wide we swept.

**Share and magnitude together.** A share answers "which group owns the variability" — but a group
can own almost all of a movement that is itself tiny. So alongside each share we report the
achievable **interquartile range** (IQR) of each of the five sub-groups when it alone is varied,
drawn as the reachable-TPC bands of @fig-decompiqr.

@fig-decompiqr is the central result: for each lever group, the family of absolute growth curves
(h⁻¹, not height-normalised) reachable by moving that group alone over the standardised $\pm H$
range, as a median curve with an interquartile band and the tuned baseline overlaid. The mapping is
visually immediate: **catalysis and allocation move the plateau height, the kinetic envelope moves
the cold-side rising limb, and the melting temperature moves the hot falling edge**.

![**Reachable growth curves per lever group (central result).** Absolute growth-rate curves (h⁻¹, not height-normalised) reachable by moving one sub-group alone over the standardised $\pm H$ range about the tuned baseline: median (solid) with interquartile band, tuned baseline dashed. Catalysis ($\sigma$/`kcat_scale`) and allocation move the plateau height; the kinetic envelope moves the cold-side limb; stability ($T_m$) moves the hot falling edge; maintenance is near-flat at the peak.](assets/figures/decomp_iqr_bands.png){#fig-decompiqr fig-pos="H" width=100%}

**What controls what** (@tbl-decomp, @fig-decomprecast):

- **The maximum growth rate is owned by magnitude.** $r_\text{max}$ has $\varphi_\text{magnitude}=0.92$
  (envelope $0.08$), and within magnitude the achievable IQR is dominated by **catalysis** (0.33 h⁻¹,
  i.e. $\sigma$/`kcat_scale`) then allocation (0.24 h⁻¹), the envelope contributing almost nothing.
  On the reconciled single pool the rate is a catalytic-capacity quantity — how much enzyme, run how
  fast — matching the elasticity where $\sigma$'s $r_\text{max}$ elasticity is $\approx1$.
- **The optimum and the upper thermal limit are owned by the envelope — specifically stability.**
  $T_\text{opt}$ ($\varphi_\text{envelope}=0.999$) and $CT_\text{max}$ ($0.994$) move almost entirely
  with the melting-temperature axis (achievable IQR $\approx2.0$ and $1.9$ °C under stability, $\approx0$
  under everything else): the rounded peak location and the hot collapse are both set by protein
  stability.
- **The cold side and the activation energy are owned by the envelope — specifically kinetics.** The
  niche width ($\varphi_\text{envelope}=0.99$) has a large kinetic IQR (6.5 °C) and $E_a$ ($0.97$) is
  kinetic-led (IQR 0.28): the rising-limb shape and slope are governed by where enzymes work best and
  how curved their turnover is.
- **The interaction is small** across every feature ($S_\text{interaction}\le0.02$): on the tuned
  model envelope and magnitude divide the labour **cleanly** — in contrast to the pre-reconciliation
  glucose-minimal analysis, where allocation and the envelope shared the optimum through a large
  interaction term.

**The answer to the separability question.** On the tuned rich model the division of labour is
**clean**: magnitude owns the rate; the envelope owns the shape and position — with protein stability
($T_m$) setting the optimum and the upper limit and the kinetic envelope setting the cold side and
$E_a$. No single lever sets the whole curve. Pinning a real organism's growth curve therefore needs
both classes of measurement: the catalytic budget (proteome allocation $\times$ in-vivo saturation)
that sets the height, and the melting proteome ($T_m$) plus kinetic envelope that set the shape.

![**Grouped variance decomposition (tuned model, rich BHI).** Shapley shares — envelope (kinetic+stability) vs magnitude (allocation+catalysis+maintenance) — per curve feature, summing to one. $r_\text{max}$ is magnitude-owned; every shape descriptor is envelope-owned; the interaction is negligible.](assets/figures/decomp_variance.png){#fig-decomprecast fig-pos="H" width=80%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-decomp
#| tbl-cap: "Grouped variance decomposition on the TUNED model (rich BHI; equal half-width H=0.2 about the tuned operating point). Envelope (φ_env) vs magnitude (φ_mag) Shapley shares with the interaction S_int; and the achievable IQR magnitude of each of the five sub-groups (kinetic/stability/allocation/catalysis/maintenance) under that sub-group alone (native units)."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

v = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/decomposition_variance.csv").set_index("descriptor")
m = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/decomposition_iqr_magnitude.csv").set_index("descriptor")
keep = [k for k in ["rmax", "Topt_C", "CTmax_C", "niche_width_C", "Ea_eV"] if k in v.index]
tab = pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": keep,
    "φ_env": v.loc[keep, "phi_envelope"].values,
    "φ_mag": v.loc[keep, "phi_magnitude"].values,
    "S_int": v.loc[keep, "S_interaction"].values,
    "IQR kin": m.loc[keep, "kinetic"].values,
    "IQR stab": m.loc[keep, "stability"].values,
    "IQR alloc": m.loc[keep, "allocation"].values,
    "IQR catal": m.loc[keep, "catalysis"].values,
    "IQR maint": m.loc[keep, "maintenance"].values,
}).round(3)
print(tab.to_markdown(index=False))
```

# Which enzymes matter, and can we learn them from growth? (control and identifiability)

**What this section does.** The previous sections grouped parameters; here we ask two
enzyme-level questions **on the tuned model at the rich operating point**. First, **which
individual enzymes control the thermal envelope** — do a few enzymes dominate, or is control
spread thinly across the whole proteome? We answer with a **control coefficient**: after a cheap
usage×sensitivity *screen* to shortlist candidate enzymes, we finite-difference each candidate's
grounded thermal parameters and record the resulting change in every envelope descriptor; the
envelope control coefficient is the largest such influence (max $|\partial\text{descriptor}/
\partial\text{param}|$ over $\{T_{\text{opt},i}, \Delta C_{p,i}\}$). Second, **could the ~7,700
per-enzyme parameters be inferred from a growth curve alone**, or do they need direct
measurement? We answer with an *identifiability* score: a parameter is identifiable if changing
it visibly changes the growth curve (a first-order proxy — a full profile-likelihood analysis
is future work).

**Thermal control is concentrated in very few enzymes** (@fig-ctrlthermal, @tbl-control): ranked by
the control coefficient, **one enzyme dominates by an order of magnitude** — acyl-[acyl-carrier-
protein] synthetase / the acyl-carrier protein (gene *acpP/aas*, UniProt `P0A6A8`, control coefficient
≈0.54) — followed at ~1/13th the influence by succinate dehydrogenase (*sdhA/sdhB*, `P07014`),
glutamine synthetase (*glnA*, `P0A9C5`) and succinyl-CoA synthetase (*sucC/sucD*, `P0A836`), with the
coefficient falling off sharply thereafter. Notably, this per-enzyme control acts almost entirely on
the **niche width** (and, weakly, $CT_\text{max}$): the control coefficient on the organismal
$T_\text{opt}$ is essentially zero for every single enzyme, because the optimum is set by whole-cell
allocation and stability, not by any one enzyme. That a handful of enzymes set the envelope is what
makes sequence-based prediction of the envelope plausible (objective O2). Computed across all 2560 enzymes × 3 parameters (7680 in total), only
**30 (0.4 %)** are identifiable from the growth curve alone, and even among the highest-control
enzymes only ~**13 %** are (@tbl-ident). At the rich tuned peak the rate is magnitude-limited
(the previous two sections), so individual enzyme thermal parameters have even *less* leverage on
growth than at a slower operating point — growth data constrain only a tiny, high-control subset,
and the remaining **~99.6 %** of per-enzyme parameters must come from independent measurement
(objective O5). This is the identifiability argument for grounding the envelope in the melting
proteome, sequence-based optima and DLTKcat rather than fitting it to growth.

![**Which enzymes control the thermal envelope.** Top enzymes ranked by the envelope **control coefficient** (the largest absolute model-computed influence of that enzyme's thermal parameters on any envelope descriptor), gene-labelled. The signal is carried by the niche-width response; per-enzyme control of the organismal $T_\text{opt}$ is ~0. One enzyme (acyl-carrier protein, `acpP`) dominates by an order of magnitude.](assets/figures/control_thermal.png){#fig-ctrlthermal fig-pos="H" width=85%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-control
#| tbl-cap: "Top enzymes by envelope CONTROL COEFFICIENT (max |∂envelope-descriptor/∂thermal-param| over {Topt_i, dCp_i}) on the tuned model, rich BHI — the model-computed influence of each enzyme's thermal parameters on the envelope. (thermal_screen — usage × thermal sensitivity — is only the candidate-screening step, not the ranking.) Identities joined locally: enzyme name from the ecModel reaction, gene from the measured proteome (b-number → gene symbol), UniProt from the ecModel; EC is not stored in this GECKO build. Deduplicated to one row per enzyme (its strongest reaction)."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

c = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/control_top_enzymes.csv")
c["gene"] = c["gene"].fillna("").replace("nan", "")
c["enzyme_name"] = c["enzyme_name"].astype(str).str.slice(0, 42)
cols = [x for x in ["display_rank", "gene", "enzyme_name", "enzyme_id", "control_coeff"] if x in c.columns]
t = c[cols].head(10).rename(columns={"display_rank": "rank", "enzyme_name": "enzyme",
                                     "enzyme_id": "UniProt", "control_coeff": "control coefficient"})
print(t.round(4).to_markdown(index=False))
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-ident
#| tbl-cap: "Identifiability of per-enzyme parameters from the growth TPC, proteome-wide (all enzymes × {Topt_i, dCp_i, kcat_i}), a first-order control-magnitude proxy: small proteome-wide, larger among the top-K control enzymes."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

idf = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/identifiability.csv")
n = len(idf); n_ident = int(idf["identifiable_from_growth"].sum())
ref = idf[idf["refined"]] if "refined" in idf.columns else idf.iloc[0:0]
n_ref = len(ref); n_ident_ref = int(ref["identifiable_from_growth"].sum()) if n_ref else 0
summ = pd.DataFrame({
    "quantity": ["parameters (enzymes × 3)", "identifiable (proteome-wide)",
                 "identifiable among top-K control enzymes", "mean identifiability score"],
    "value": [n, f"{n_ident}/{n} ({n_ident/n:.1%})",
              f"{n_ident_ref}/{n_ref} ({n_ident_ref/n_ref:.1%})" if n_ref else "n/a",
              round(idf["ident"].mean(), 3)]})
print(summ.to_markdown(index=False))
```

# Interpretation and caveats

Control of the growth curve on the tuned model is **cleanly feature-specific**. **The magnitude
levers set the maximum rate** — on the reconciled single pool $r_\text{max}$ is owned by catalysis
and allocation ($\varphi_\text{magnitude}=0.92$; the in-vivo saturation $\sigma$ has an $r_\text{max}$
elasticity of $\approx1$), not by the envelope. **Protein stability — the melting temperature
$T_m$ — sets the optimum and the upper thermal limit** ($T_\text{opt}$ and $CT_\text{max}$ both
$\varphi_\text{envelope}\approx0.99$, essentially all stability). **The kinetic envelope sets the
cold-side shape and the activation energy.** The interaction is small, so — unlike a slower
operating point — the labour divides cleanly: magnitude owns the height, the envelope owns the
shape and position. Thermal control is concentrated in a few enzymes, yet **over 99 % of per-enzyme
parameters cannot be inferred from the growth curve alone** — even more so at the magnitude-limited
rich peak — which is why we ground them in independent data (the melting proteome, sequence-based
optima, DLTKcat and temperature proteomics) rather than fitting them to growth. Grounding the
allocation layer *costs* some empirical growth-curve fit (an ablation drops the fit $R^2$ from 0.74
with the enzyme envelope alone to 0.49 with the full sectors), so its value is the proteome realism
and the ability to attribute control to measurable quantities, not a better fit.

**The magnitude story — mostly closed, and honestly framed.** The emergent model reproduced the
*shape* of the exact-strain curve a priori but under-predicted the absolute rich peak; the
calibration closes most of that gap — the posterior-predictive peak rises from $\approx1.04$ to
$2.14$ h⁻¹ [1.98, 2.29], about **89 %** of the way to the observed 2.40 — with a modest turnover
correction (`kcat_scale` 1.25) and the in-vivo saturation $\sigma=0.867$ railed toward its physical
1.0 ceiling. **This overturns an earlier reading of a "structural" ceiling.** In an intermediate
build the rich peak appeared capped near $1.55$ h⁻¹ regardless of the magnitude levers; the ceiling
diagnostic traced this to a **proteome double-accounting artefact** — two redundant enzyme-mass
pools (the sMOMENT sector pool and a leftover GECKO base `prot_pool_exchange` bound), so the levers
saturated against a pool they could not touch. Reconciling the two into a single binding budget
(the pool-reconciliation and ceiling-diagnostic notes) removed the artefact, and the magnitude
levers then move the rate as the elasticity now shows. The **remaining** $2.14\!\to\!2.40$ residual
is not structural either: it is the $\sigma\le1$ **physical ceiling**. Read plainly, the rich peak
demands **near-maximal in-vivo enzyme saturation** — *E. coli* operating above the yeast-derived
0.4–0.5 average, the fast-grower point (Li's yeast sat comfortably at $\sigma=0.5$ and was never
stress-tested against a 3–5× faster organism; 2.40 h⁻¹ is a ~17-min doubling, a real high-end
rich-medium rate, and the digitized observed value is itself a high-end point). The soft caveat is
that a parameter pinned to its bound signals the **metabolic budget ($P_\text{total}\times
f_\text{metab}$) is tight at the peak** — and the coupled growth law deliberately shifts proteome
to ribosomes (shrinks $f_\text{metab}$) exactly at fast growth, so part of the residual is a
growth-law allocation squeeze at the rich peak. This is a concrete, quantified modelling frontier —
the rich-medium metabolic budget under the growth law — not an unexplained failure.

Remaining limitations, all structural / in-silico: **(i)** the rising-limb curvature is set by a
single literature $\Delta C_p$ prior, so per-enzyme curvature (from broader DLTKcat coverage) is the
route to a more accurate cold side; **(ii)** the chaperone/stress sector is imposed as a maintenance
input, not modelled as flux-carrying reactions; **(iii)** identifiability is a first-order proxy
(profile-likelihood is future work); **(iv)** parameters and proteome are *E. coli*-specific
(per-strain proteomes / predictors are the library-scale route); **(v)** growth only — respiration
and carbon-use efficiency are future work [@Machado2021]; **(vi)** the calibration uses a single
figure-digitized rich curve — an MG1655 defined-minimal TPC remains the gold-standard next test.

# Next steps

Prioritised (✓ scaffolded, ○ future): 1. **Per-enzyme $\Delta C_p$ curvature calibration**
to bring $E_a$ to the conserved value while keeping the $T_m$-set $CT_\text{max}$ (○).
2. **An explicit temperature-dependent chaperone/stress sector** with flux-carrying
folding load, beyond the current maintenance-input treatment (○). 3. **Profile-likelihood
identifiability** replacing the first-order proxy (○). 4. **Respiration and carbon-use-
efficiency TPCs** (○). 5. **Scale across the isolate library** with per-strain proteomes
and sequence-based $T_\text{opt}$/$T_m$ predictors to estimate the envelope-vs-allocation
control across taxa (○). 6. **Hierarchical-Bayesian calibration** of the remaining
free couplings against phenotype/proteome/flux data [@Li2021; @Pettersen2023] (○).
